author: @robertlivingston360
text: I think it is time to discuss impedance matching. Power sources have inherent resistive losses, so if you vary the loading, the efficiency follows a plotted power exchange curve. Too little loading means that you are not utilizing all the available power. Too much loading means that you are losing more power in the source resistance than in the load. There is a nifty mathematical graph to show this. The same idea can be applied to the actual generation process. If a wind turbine just free wheels, then power is not being fully utilized. Too much loading and the turbine stalls, Turns out that if the turbine is loaded such that it slows to about half the speed  of free wheeling then you are at the best power exchange point. But how to know what the free wheeling speed is when being actually used. The answer is a pilot generator that is a scale model of the larger. As the wind changes, the loading can be made to automatically adjust, maintaining the best efficiency. A MPPE solar panel regulator does this by taking periodic and very fast open circuit voltage samples to track available sunshine.

replies.author: @littlegandhi1199
replies.text:
AC - Alternating Current (house  mains) electricity alternately flows to and from the end point

DC - Direct Current (batteries) the electricity only flows through the battery and the end device in one direction. Positive and negative are important.

He showed a schematic to a bridge rectifier which was using 4 diodes (diodes prevent electricity from flowing a certain direction) and is a common technique to catch the backwards electricity and re orient the right way again to make it usable as DC to charge to a battery or run many common electronics (which all take AC out of the wall) and do their own bridge rectification to get to DC before their electronics use it that way.

This is also why if we have our own batteries @ the right voltage already and since it's DC we can throw away that brick that plugs into the wall and feed DC directly to cameras, microphones, etc etc

author: @Patriarchtech
text: Robert you should probably mention that the regulators (switch mode dc/dc power supplies)mostly are either step down(buck) or step up (boost) there are combination ones called a buck boost converters/regulators. This is important because as a buck converter can only output a lower voltage on the output of what it receives on the input. A boost module the opposite. So you want to keep that in mind when you buy it. Typically you know what voltage you need on your output. If you need to charge a LIFEPO4 24v 8s battery for example you need something like 28-28.4v volts on the output of your regulator, but if your source provides 12volts you would want a boost regulator converter. If you source provides 50v you would want the buck version. Buck boost regulators do exist but are usually more difficult to find and they are more expensive but they will do both.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good point mate - thanks for adding it

replies.author: @Patriarchtech
replies.text: I Love Switched mode DC/DC power supplies. They are very efficient up to 97%. Often though they don't come with reverse voltage protection. I cannot stress enough how much you have to take this into consideration especially in charging circuits. A battery is a power supply and so is your voltage regulator. As you know  Voltage and current flows from high to low. In other words you need to use diodes.

author: @Dave_D.
text: There are some very inexpensive MPPT's out there as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: MPPT is just about battery charging mate - it's a block you add between the voltage regulator and the battery pack

replies.author: @Dave_D.
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Well, that's true, but I thought that's what most of this was about....how to stabilize your rectified DC and use it for something.  So if you want to charge a battery, and your generated voltage swings wildly, MPPT will maintain your volts and adjust the amps going to the battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Dave_D. Oh - i didn't think of it that way - I tend to see batteries as on application - plus MPPT isn't really needed - it's used - as is PWM as is nothing at all - MPPT gives more control - that's for sure - and sometimes you need to control battery charging and discharging like if you use lithium - but we might be getting into the topic of another video right here lol - i tried to keep it to absolute basics but i like the comments section when more complicated stuff comes out there and gets discussed - that sort of makes it for everyone in my mind - cheers mate and thanks for raising the point

replies.author: @Dave_D.
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I guess I was thinking that MPPT WAS simple, in that, our generators would be the same variable type output as wind or solar.  The advantage of MPPT over the regulator is that you maintain the wattage as well...w/ MPPT, the input could be 100v @ 1A, but if you are charging 12v batteries, you'd get 14v (or whatever charge voltage you are using per stage) @ 7A.  Maybe I'm wrong, but wouldn't the regulator as you have shown 'waste' power by reducing say 20v to 5v (though allow you to maintain a constant 5v)?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Dave_D. my understanding of MPPT might differ from yours mate - in my understanding MPPT is the basically the same as PWM but PWM has a fixed duty cycle whereas in MPPT the duty cycle is variable so the impedance of the source is matched to the impedance of the load at any one time at which point maximum power is transferred  - so, in essence it is really just switching. It is really a DC to DC conversion and as solar panels are already DC then there is no issue but as other forms of generation tend to be AC then DC conversion has to be done first

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
Great one Rob! This will be a good starting place for a great many.

I too tend to forget to explain some of the "basics" as I've been tinkering with electrical appliances and electronics since shortly after I could walk. We were broke, we rarely missed a meal but it was often made from only what our Church's pantry could supply that week so my toys were, and were made from, what appliances my parents found at the side of the road. 
I assume it's largely the same in most places that things left at the roadside are an invitation for anyone to take and it's considered courteous to leave a simple note of "works" or "broken" and for the taker to take the entire appliance so as to not leave a mess. Please, those reading, how common is this?
We aren't so bad off now (though I knew no difference growing up) but I'm still bringing interesting things home and having my children take things apart, see how they work or why they don't and if it's worth fixing or harvesting for parts. I don't want them to miss out on the best part of learning by giving it a go.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's what i am hoping for mate - and there is no better way to learn than by doing for sure

author: @jonesPossibly
text: what about MPPT solar controllers.  do they work in a similar way to the whole setup here?

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: wouldnt their input already be DC from the solar panel?

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text:
The topic of MPPT fills more than a couple volumes but the basics are that using a control loop it uses a variable DC-DC converter to "test" the Wattage (Volts times Amps) at the range of it's input Voltage to find the "best" setting.
Some algorithms are better at one application, some at another. That's where you can really dig in if you're interested.

I hope that made sense and was helpful.

replies.author: @jonesPossibly
replies.text: @matthewellisor5835 so you would need to rectify the voltage before putting them through an mppt controller?

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text: @grendel1960a Or from a rectified AC source. Some even have internal rectifiers to do the job and to protect from being incorrectly installed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: logically MPPT is really just a battery charging regime mate - it sits between the voltage regulator and the batteries - but it is not strictly necessary - as a circuit it tends to be used in solar as it can't really cope well with spikes that can arise in wind and often on an MPPT board you see rectification and regulation included
